questions.question: Derive the formula for work done when a force acts at an angle θ to the displacement.
questions.answer: Work done, W = F × d × cosθ, where only the component of force along the displacement does work.

questions.question: Calculate the work done when a force of 10 N moves an object 5 m in the direction of the force.
questions.answer: Work done, W = F × d = 10 × 5 = 50 J.

questions.question: An object of mass 2 kg moves with a velocity of 3 m/s. Find its kinetic energy.
A body of mass 5 kg is lifted to a height of 4 m. Calculate its potential energy.
If a body is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s, what will be its kinetic energy if its mass is 3 kg?
A man pushes a cart with a force of 20 N for 10 m at an angle of 60° to the horizontal. Calculate the work done.
questions.answer: Work done, W = F × d × cosθ = 20 × 10 × cos60° = 200 × 0.5 = 100 J.

questions.question: Define power and derive its formula in terms of work done and time.
questions.answer: Power = Rate of doing work = Work / Time; P = W / t.

questions.question: An electric motor does 500 J of work in 10 s. Calculate the power developed.
Explain the difference between kinetic energy and potential energy with examples.
questions.answer: Kinetic energy is due to motion (e.g., moving car), while potential energy is due to position (e.g., water at height).

questions.question: State the law of conservation of energy and give an example.
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it only changes form. Example: A pendulum's potential energy converts to kinetic energy and back.

questions.question: A body falls freely from a height of 20 m. Calculate its velocity just before hitting the ground (g = 10 m/s²).
Define negative work and give one example.
questions.answer: Negative work occurs when force opposes displacement. Example: Friction acting on a moving block.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is moving with a speed of 5 m/s. What is its kinetic energy? If it comes to rest, how much work is done by friction?
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 × 2 × 5² = 25 J. Work done by friction = 25 J.

questions.question: Explain with an example how work done results in energy change.
questions.answer: When a person lifts a weight, work done by the person increases the potential energy of the weight.

questions.question: Derive the formula for total mechanical energy of a body.
questions.answer: Total mechanical energy = KE + PE = 1/2 mv² + mgh.

questions.question: A car of mass 1000 kg is moving at 20 m/s. Find its kinetic energy.
A body of mass 10 kg is lifted to a height of 5 m. Calculate work done against gravity.
questions.answer: Work done = PE = m × g × h = 10 × 9.8 × 5 ≈ 490 J.

questions.question: State and explain one real-life application of work and energy.
questions.answer: Hydroelectric power plants convert potential energy of water into electrical energy.

questions.question: A body moves 10 m under a horizontal force of 50 N. Calculate the work done.
questions.answer: Work done, W = F × d = 50 × 10 = 500 J.

questions.question: Explain why no work is done when you push a wall without moving it.
questions.answer: Work = Force × displacement. Displacement is zero, so work done is zero.

questions.question: A spring stores 10 J of energy when stretched. What type of energy is this?
questions.answer: It is potential energy.

questions.question: Derive the formula connecting work done and kinetic energy change.
questions.answer: Work done = ΔKE = KE(final) - KE(initial).

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards. Describe the changes in kinetic and potential energy.
questions.answer: As it rises, KE decreases and PE increases. At the top, KE = 0 and PE is maximum. On falling, PE converts back to KE.

questions.question: A force of 30 N acts on an object at an angle of 90° to its displacement. Calculate the work done.
questions.answer: Work done W = F × d × cos90° = 0 J.

questions.question: Explain how mechanical energy is conserved in a frictionless pendulum.
questions.answer: Potential energy at the highest point converts into kinetic energy at the lowest point and back, keeping total mechanical energy constant.
